nodes.name: 稀树草原
nodes.summary: 东非常见的开放草地与湿地边缘复合景观。

nodes.name: 撒哈拉以南非洲
nodes.summary: 撒哈拉以南非洲是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联90种鸟类，包括草黃樹鴨、埃及雁、Western crested guinea fowl等90种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 阿拉伯半岛
nodes.summary: 阿拉伯半岛是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联45种鸟类，包括Harlequin quail、Arabian partridge、Arabian bustard等45种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: EN
nodes.summary: EN表示 IUCN 濒危等级，当前关联384种鸟类，如朱鹮、中华秋沙鸭、黑脸琵鹭等384种鸟类。

nodes.name: 苏丹
nodes.summary: 苏丹是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联94种鸟类，包括南非鸭、Sand partridge、Scaly spurfowl等94种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 吉布提
nodes.summary: 吉布提是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联29种鸟类，包括索马里鸵鸟、Yellow-necked spurfowl、Arabian bustard等29种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 农药污染
nodes.summary: 农业化学品通过水体和食物链影响鸟类生存。

nodes.name: 刚果民主共和国
nodes.summary: 刚果民主共和国是图谱中的地点节点，当前关联274种鸟类，包括南非鸭、Black guinea fowl、剛果孔雀等274种鸟类，主要用于表示这些物种的分布地、停歇地或观测地。

nodes.name: 埃塞俄比亚

nodes.name: 短尾雕
nodes.summary: 短尾雕（Bateleur，学名 Terathopius ecaudatus），在图谱中标记为濒危（EN）物种，已关联19个分布地点节点，常见栖息地包括稀树草原，主要威胁包括栖息地破坏、农药污染、人类活动扰动。

nodes.name: 人类活动扰动
nodes.summary: 旅游、施工和高频活动影响繁殖或觅食。

nodes.summary: 开发、采伐和围垦等活动导致适宜生境减少。

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Sandgrouse, 17, 54-72. In central and east Africa, the bateleur may be found in northern Cameroon, southern Niger, southern Chad, southern Sudan, South Sudan, northern Central African Republic, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Djibouti, western Somalia, northern, eastern and southern Democratic Republic of the Congo and a majority of Uganda, Kenya and Tanzania.Nikolaus, G

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Routledge. In Ethiopia, it tends to be associated with well-wooded areas. Habitat tends to be most closely studied in southern Africa. It is mostly common found in broad-leaved woodland in the Okavango Delta in Botswana

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: In Namibia it is often found over tall woodland near drainage lines, and over ephemeral rivers in north-eastern Namibia and within the more arid Etosha National Park. In Zambia, it is found in a variety of habitats from woodlands to open plains but avoids the most densely wooded areas. Reportedly in Malawi, it is often associated with Forest–savanna mosaics but is sometimes regularly seen over cultivated areas and even may be seen flying over large cities. To the contrary, in Mozambique it is said to avoid areas with a dense human population.Parker, V

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: In Namibia it is often found over tall woodland near drainage lines, and over ephemeral rivers in north-eastern Namibia and within the more arid Etosha National Park. In Zambia, it is found in a variety of habitats from woodlands to open plains but avoids the most densely wooded areas. Reportedly in Malawi, it is often associated with Forest–savanna mosaics but is sometimes regularly seen over cultivated areas and even may be seen flying over large cities. To the contrary, in Mozambique it is said to avoid areas with a dense human population.Parker, V

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: thumb|right|An adult and juvenile in characteristic savanna habitat in [[Botswana.]] The bateleur is a common to fairly common resident or nomadic bird of the partially open savanna country and of woodland within Sub-Saharan Africa. During breeding, it tends to require closed-canopy savannah-woodland habitats, including Acacia savanna as well as mopane and miombo woodlands

links.label: 保护等级
links.evidence: status = EN

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: Gabar, 6: 3-6. The declines of the species are almost entirely due to anthropogenic causes. These include but are not limited to habitat destruction, the poisoning of carcasses, persecution through shooting and possibly pesticide use.Ogada, D

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: Gabar, 6: 3-6. The declines of the species are almost entirely due to anthropogenic causes. These include but are not limited to habitat destruction, the poisoning of carcasses, persecution through shooting and possibly pesticide use.Ogada, D

links.label: 受威胁于
links.evidence: Ostrich, 71(1-2), 25-32. Additionally, shrinking habitat has been found to be a prevalent threat to bateleurs due largely to expanding human settlements and intensifying livestock agriculture. A further effect from humans is regular disturbance at bateleur nests, although not typically as deliberate as many other threats, this is causing the breeding success rates to plummet farther. No large scale actions are underway but they are possibly protected in Yemen as an endangered species. It is proposed to implement education and awareness campaigns across its range to reduce the use of poisoned baits
